speaker: interviewer
text: --six. Jacob, we just got to the point where the war started and you were taken into the military.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just before that, what happened at the sanitarium where you were working? What happened to the administration?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, because when I went to the-- to the [INAUDIBLE] it was cold. So it was in a rush. I had to just in a few hours come to the-- to the company, my company. So I didn't know what's happened to them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And just-- just men in the army just went to the-- to the place when they collect the old soldiers. They give uniforms and rifles and everything. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you been in the army before? Had you done military service?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, everybody does military service, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? How old were you when-- when you did that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it for two years?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what company were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: 21. 21 [POLISH] was it called. They called us [POLISH] means children of Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And your brother-- was he in the same company?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was not. He was in a different company. He was in the-- in the motorized company, when he ride the motorbike in the company.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And what did they equip you with? What sort of weapons did you have, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a rifle. And what's it called? To dig-- to dig trenches-- a little--

speaker: interviewer
text: A space?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is a spade, yes. It's a spade and a bayonet, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. And where did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They take me from Warsaw. From the Warsaw citadel, we went with a very strong, fast march to Mlawa. Mlawa was close-- close to the-- it's north, north of Warsaw, about 80 kilometers from Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many-- how many of you in that company?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and dozens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there Jews and non-Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, yeah, of course, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you arrived in Mlawa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well-- well, after Mlawa, we have [INAUDIBLE], [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the blisters on the foot, and we're very tire. And we start straightaway when we come. We have to go to attack. And the old fields was open. The corn was in the-- the-- in the-- what would make the fields [POLISH], what they call in Polish. It was burned. They burned this, and we go to attack and-- and suddenly we weren't-- we hear on the left side and right side hurrah. Hurrah, [POLISH]. And they-- they attacked the-- the trenches, the German trenches. And-- and after a while, when they was shooting, it was quiet. And the officers in the army, they keep all, and say, back, back, back, back, back, back-- to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: They told you to retreat?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- to-- what means retreat, to go back?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, to get back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, yes. And were a lot of the Poles killed? Did they kill a lot of your army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh [INAUDIBLE]. They kill a lot. But you never know, because every-- every-- every is thinking about his own life. And you-- you don't look much at around. You just-- when they said you have to run, you just run.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- were you hurt at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- no, I was not hurt, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. And where did you run to?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] run back. It was very chaotic because nobody knows where to go and what to do. And when you hear it in the night other soldiers tell you they-- they bombard their own-- own kitchen. They bombarded their own kitchens. They didn't know. It was very chaotic. And-- and when you run, you don't-- you don't look or anything. You just start to-- to run [LAUGHS] because this is your life. And you have to run. So I-- I remember when I run, there was a few officers in the-- in the crowd. I think there was a produce wagon. And I jump on the back-- on the back bumper bar. I jump on the back bumper, and I couldn't-- I haven't got to-- to keep my hands to-- to say. So I turned to [? Burnett, ?] and [LAUGHS] I make a hole in the roof. And I keep my hands-- they didn't know what happened. They was afraid somebody wants to kill us. Yes, it was just all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- where did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They take you to-- to back. I remember the-- the name of the-- of the of the cities or the-- the villages. I don't remember the names.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went back to Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to Warsaw, yes. I went back to Warsaw after two or three years. And the most was they make the-- they want to make them orderly, collect the soldiers to put them on the-- on the-- on the-- on the highway. Right It was huge from both sides. And they keep them [INAUDIBLE] and the-- the German Luftwaffe bombard it, and makes a mess, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Most of them were killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not most, but many. I don't know because you never know. Of course, there was many killed, but you see killed soldiers, but you don't know how many. But you see killed soldiers, horses, and everything mixed up the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened, Jacob, when you got back to Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got back to Warsaw, they start, again, organize the soldiers to keep some [POLISH], as the call to protect Warsaw from the Nazis. And I was in the Warsaw squadron. It was close to what they call [POLISH] applies. And we-- we-- we sit there and-- with a few rifles. And we want to-- to-- to stops them. But you know, the houses in-- in-- in-- in-- in [INAUDIBLE] but the houses are not compact together, but different houses. And they live under the houses to-- to have some protection and shoot them. And-- and-- in a few nights, we attacked, but they-- they-- they-- they have prepared items, have prepared the [NON-ENGLISH] what they call the special weapons. But they shoot, and they-- and they-- and they-- and they shoot out, and the shrapnel, they fell after the houses. It was-- yes, but, so they have to give up, and-- and they take us to-- to leave-- the-- the rifles, to leave everything. And they put in different places, and leave everything and go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you got home?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got home, I say we were going to run from the place. When I come home. it was destroyed, was burnt, and-- and-- but everybody survived. And they went to my brother's place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went there. Everybody was there, yes. And what-- what could you do then? What decision did you make?

speaker: interviewee
text: I make a decision to-- to-- to go away, to run away, because to stay in Warsaw means to go every day to-- to-- to clean the streets. They take from the-- from the ghetto. It was not a ghetto at this time when I run. But they collect the Jews, and-- and they-- they have to clean the-- the-- the city. And yes, my wife has an uncle in Brzesc. It's-- it's the other side of the the-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- means the border between-- between-- between German and Russia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So Brzesc was on the Russian side, and she has an uncle there. So we decide to go there. And how did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was through-- through the borders [INAUDIBLE]. And when-- when we-- [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We find a peasant with a-- with a carriage and a horse, and he take us to the border. And we go through the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what happened on the border?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the border, yes, there was a German-- German-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Guard?

speaker: interviewee
text: A German guard, yes. And they-- they asked me where we're going. We are from Brzesc. And we're going to Brzesc. And they search me [INAUDIBLE] and my wife. They search my wife. And we went. And they took everything that we have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: They say-- my wife is Jewish, they say. But you are not Jewish. You stay up here to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they say that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. They didn't recognize me as Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: They thought you were Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And what sort of things did you have with you? Did you have money, or did you have other clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We have-- we have-- we have money that they take from us. And I have-- and I have a bottle of vodka that they take from me and some jewelry [INAUDIBLE] my wife they took.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they let you keep your clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, they didn't take those.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took that too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clothes? No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you got to your uncle-- to your wife's uncle in Brzesc?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we went to her uncle, he-- he runs a shop in Brest. And-- and this time it was-- it was-- the-- it was how to say-- a pact-- a pact there, an understanding between the Hitler and-- and Stalin, So they take Poland to [POLISH]. [POLISH] means the river, the border. And the Russians take the parts, and German the this part.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that part of the world then became part of Russia-- Soviet Union?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my-- her uncle decide to go away to-- he liquidate his shop. And he decide to go away. And he-- he told us he was going. He find some people who can smuggle him to Japan and through Japan to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was he successful in doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did he want you to go too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he [INAUDIBLE]. Yes, but then we didn't go. My wife was pregnant and-- and everything. We think it would be better if we stay. But then we stay and start the-- the war with Russia-- Germany with Russia start the war. So we have to go somewhere. We went to Pinsk. It's a small city, many Jews. And from Pinsk, we went to-- I was working there a while, and from Pinsk, we went to Janow Podlaski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where is this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in between Pinsk and-- and-- and-- and [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- was it Soviet Union?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was Soviet Union, because-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that-- is that Bala, Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that Bala, Russia? Bala, Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is it Belarus? Yes, Belarus, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you speak Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I couldn't speak the Russian at this time. But I learned fast, yes, later on, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you manage to find work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, oh yes, yes. I find work, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of work was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] yes, I find, yes. I was-- in Pinsk, I was working. It was a-- a-- [INAUDIBLE]-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] a nursery, a [INAUDIBLE] nursery, like a-- like a [INAUDIBLE]-- like a [INAUDIBLE] nursery. And it was a Pole yelling. His name-- and he run with the German. So it was a post up here. And I think over the nursery in Pinsk. And from Pinsk, I went to-- to Janow Podlaski. In Janow Podlaski, I work for a Russian-- Russian institution. It was called [NON-ENGLISH] means a-- a special [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] company who runs-- who runs farms and, and the all produce-- distributed all produce for the-- for the-- for the company.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it like a cooperative?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was state. I don't think that a cooperative is state. It's run by the state, not cooperative. And yes, I was in a-- a good position, because I have to travel a lot because we haven't got seeds. And I have to travel a lot to Ukraine direction to collect some seeds from the farmers, to buy seeds, because I haven't got seeds. And and I was carrying a lot of money with me, and I was buying seeds and sending to-- to-- to-- to-- Janow Podlaski. And-- and-- and where I have the possibility to go a lot, I-- I-- I visit my brother in Lvov.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Andrew.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Andrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I visit him in Lvov, and we've been a few hours or dine together. And I told him what I am doing, and-- and I then went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he tell you what was happening with your parents and your sisters back in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he didn't know. He just left them and his place and went away, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had no news what was happening in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely not, no news, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't know about the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we know about the ghetto, but we didn't know what's happened, and who is in the ghetto. No, absolutely not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever send letters back, or any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we sent letters. We sent letters, but I never receive a letter. No. And was your wife working too? Yes, she was working too. Yes, she was always working, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- when was the baby born?

speaker: interviewee
text: The baby was born in in-- in '39. No, '39 was the war-- yes, in '40.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me the story about the farm you worked on, about the German who worked on the farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I was working in a-- in a farm around the time that the German come. Yes, this was before they destroyed the ghetto. Yes, I used to work still in the German when they come in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One day, they collect-- I didn't see them because I was in the field, but later on when I come in the-- in the-- in the-- into town, they told me it was a group of German SS who it was. And they-- they took the-- the men-- only man. They took over 100 men And between all the men was my-- my-- my wife's father. And he-- he-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and yes, and they took them. They took them, and they-- and they rode. And-- and they shoot them. Later on, I saw the-- the peasant with carriages. They-- they-- they carried the bodies in the carriages somewhere. I don't know where they took them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you go to live in the ghetto? When did they make the ghetto in Janow Podlaski?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just went the come. Just-- it was-- '42, I think it was. '41, '42, yes, when they start organize the ghetto, and they surround the ghetto with-- with wire. And-- and-- and-- and this time, I was in field, and I come back. On the road when I go back, some-- some citizen sitting and told me, don't go there, because the ghetto is surrounded. And they-- they-- they-- they shoot everybody. Don't go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how long-- how long had you been in the ghetto? How long had you been in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in the ghetto a few months. I don't remember exactly how long.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were able to go out to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I take to work, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Did most of the men do that or only some of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But this was-- was-- the Germans didn't take too to do work. Everybody was working somewhere for the Russians. And still, the whole-- the whole structure of the-- of the work was the same. Again, everybody was going on before to work. And-- but-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yes. And when I come back, and I meet some-- a Jew with a boy. And we go together to the ghetto. And the ghetto was burned. It was on fire. And we stay in a shed opposite-- opposite the street in a shed. Here was a local person. He know exactly everything, because he was born there. He lived his whole life in Janow Podlaski in the-- in the little town. So he knows everything. And He told me, come with me. And I went with him. And we went in the shed. And we just stayed in the shed until late night. And we saw the ghetto burning. And we saw the-- the-- the machine guns around. And the shot-- we heard shots and burning the ghetto, and shots [INAUDIBLE] because the people are running from the fire. And they shot everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife and child were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later on, he say, come with me I know-- I know here a person, because he made a business with some local people, and then-- and then-- called Hala. Come with me. He'll give us a place to stay overnight and tomorrow [INAUDIBLE] what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went-- I went with him. And he opened the shed and put us in the shed. And in the straw, we lie in the straw, and we lie through the night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the-- in the early morning, we hear, like, soldiers are walking on the-- on the-- on the stones, because everything was stones in the-- the yard. And we hear the soldiers are coming. And he opened the-- the-- the gate-- the gate. And they are staying with the-- with the rifles and the bayonets. [GERMAN]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: In the column, we were walking in a column, and this time the-- the people who run away, they find. They collect, and they take them in the marketplace, and they shoot in the marketplace. And when we are walking-- I don't know how many, maybe 20 people or less are walking-- somebody, a soldier, or a policeman are coming, and say, halt. Don't take them to the marketplace. They're finished there. This place is finished. Take them to the-- to the-- to the-- to the arrest-- to the-- to the jail. It was a little jail-- two-- two-- two rooms and a corridor-- a little jail. And they put us in the jail. They put us in the jail. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] and I-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was sitting in the corridor. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I look up. And I aw-- and I saw the loft. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I think I'll jump in the loft. And I went, people, help me. Help me to go in. And I went in the loft.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was sitting here, and I think how people are, oddly enough, honest not to say to the German somebody run. But nobody did say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you the only one who went up there? [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry. No, a boy-- a boy come. A boy come over. [DRINKS] Not a boy-- a young man. I don't know him. I didn't know him. [INAUDIBLE]. And he come, and we sit, both of us. And nobody else want to-- to-- to jump in. So we-- we look around, what to do, how to run from here. [CRYING] And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] I think it is a little [INAUDIBLE] like a window from one side, from the backyard And I think this could be the way we can run. But it was very high. So I think the-- the vice. We took the vice, and with the vice we can do something to jump. And it was dark. And we see through the-- through the planks in the wall. We see when they take everybody from the jail outside in-- in the garden. And-- and they take-- take-- they tell them to take their clothes off. They take-- and then they take the clothes. We are sitting and looking at everything what's happening there. And they take the clothes. And they took them away without the clothes, just to-- to prepare before they have the trenches with-- with-- with-- with-- [NON-ENGLISH], [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] yes, ready to bury. And it was quiet. Nobody was there. And so I think we wouldn't jump through the window. And I have a look through the-- through the-- through the little door. Look down, and nobody's there. And I said, I jump when somebody's standing, I jump on him from the back, and I-- maybe I can take the rifle from him. Oh, but when I jump down, and I say to him nobody's here. Nobody's here. And I said, come on. Come down. And we go down, and I tell him not to run. And we walk. We walk back in the backyard. And we go through the back yard to the main road-- to the main road.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody-- nobody saw you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody saw us. One saw us because the [INAUDIBLE] place where they collect the milk from the-- from the-- they call this a milk collection place, where they collect milk from-- from the-- from the peasants. And there was somebody standing. He saw us. He saw us. But he didn't know he was looking. He maybe didn't know it was Jews running. But just we go. We went.
